trajectory_summary: The call opens on a soft disqualifier (existing solar) and the rep makes a reasonable pivot to batteries, but the call never builds real energy — it drifts through a long bill-collection slog, a missed emotional anchor on the kWh read, a missed empathy beat on the cancer disclosure, and a cost objection handled with reassurance rather than inversion. The appointment is booked, but it is structurally fragile: cost anxiety is unresolved, the cancer/doctor-appointment context makes the Wednesday slot uncertain, and no button-up elements beyond time and phone number were confirmed.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (Wednesday 1:30 PM), so a 'booked' outcome tag would be technically correct. However, the appointment is conditionally fragile — John's cost anxiety was not resolved, his health situation introduces schedule volatility, and the button-up was incomplete (no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no one-hour block stated). A 'booked' tag without a fragility flag overstates the quality of this outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I have to check to see if I have any doctor's appointments. I got cancer.
primary_coaching_focus.why: John disclosed a cancer diagnosis and the rep moved directly to scheduling logistics. On a call where the appointment is already fragile (cost anxiety, uncertain schedule, incomplete button-up), the empathy miss is the single highest-risk factor for a no-show — John has no emotional reason to keep this appointment. The schema is explicit: vulnerability disclosures are trust gifts. The rep needs to practice the acknowledge-and-bridge sequence so it becomes reflexive, not an afterthought.

key_moments.quote: Were you looking at battery backups or expanding your system?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep pivots to batteries immediately after John discloses existing solar — a reasonable instinct — but skips the structured orphaned_flow sequence entirely. The schema requires: own vs. lease, company name, panel size, system working. Rep asked own-vs-lease (01:38) and company (01:51) but never asked panel size or whether the system is currently working. The system-working question is a hard-DQ trigger per the schema. Skipping it means the rep booked an appointment without confirming the lead is eligible.

John reads his monthly kWh usage (1,730 kWh) and the rep responds 'K.' — canonical capture-and-drop. The schema's bill_anchor_depth directive is explicit: capture is necessary but not sufficient. The rep needed to flip this number into emotional alignment — 1,730 kWh monthly on a grid-tied system with a battery gap is the exact argument for why a Powerwall matters. Instead, the rep moves immediately to requesting a photo of the bill. The emotional leverage of the number is never used.

key_moments.quote: I have to check to see if I have any doctor's appointments. I got cancer.
key_moments.stewart_read: John discloses a cancer diagnosis mid-scheduling. The rep's response is 'Yeah. K. Well, let's let's get an idea of what that schedule looks like.' The schema classifies vulnerability disclosures as trust gifts — the correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value. The rep acknowledged the scheduling constraint but not the human moment. The call's emotional temperature drops here and does not recover.

key_moments.quote: if it involves a lot of money, I don't have no time.
key_moments.stewart_read: John's cost objection is direct and linked to his health situation — he's signaling that his bandwidth for financial decisions is limited. The rep responds with 'it really is super simple and easy to make switch. It doesn't cost a ton.' This engages the objection on its own terms (cost reassurance) rather than inverting. The inversion available here: the battery's value is in what it protects against — John's panels are already covered in ice and not producing; the cost of NOT having backup is the grid dependency he's already experiencing. The rep had the material for this flip (John's own ice/snow complaint at 15:17 was already foreshadowed in the call's context) but did not use it.
